factual-accuracy.requirement: States combined federal+Ontario marginal tax rate at $95k income is 31%-32% or identifies bracket approximately $93k-$106k
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state a combined federal+Ontario marginal tax rate of 31–32% at $95k, nor does it identify the bracket as approximately $93k–$106k. Instead, it claims an approximate combined marginal tax rate near 43.4% around that income level.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Calculates $1,000 RRSP contribution saves approximately $310-$320 in tax at 31%-32% marginal rate
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not calculate the tax savings on a $1,000 RRSP contribution at a 31%–32% marginal rate, nor does it state the ~$310–$320 savings figure. Instead, it uses an estimated ~43.4% marginal rate and gives an example of $17,100 × 43.4% ≈ $7,421.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States 2025 RRSP annual dollar limit is $32,490
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state that the 2025 RRSP annual dollar limit is $32,490. It instead mentions a 2026 RRSP limit of $33,810 and calculates RRSP room based on 18% of income, so the required 2025 figure is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States RRSP contribution room is 18% of prior year earned income (lesser of percentage or dollar limit plus carry-forward)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the RRSP contribution limit is the lesser of 18% of prior year earned income or a dollar maximum, and it also notes unused room can be carried forward.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Calculates 18% of $95,000 yields approximately $17,000-$17,100 in new RRSP contribution room
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly calculates 18% of $95,000 as $17,100 and describes this as the RRSP room amount, which falls within the required ~$17,000–$17,100 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG provides 20% grant on first $2,500 contributed per child per year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the CESG provides an annual grant of 20% on up to $2,500 of RESP contributions per child (i.e., up to $500 per year), which matches the criterion.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG annual maximum is $500 per child per year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the CESG is “20% of up to $2,500 contribution = maximum $500 grant per year per child,” which matches the required annual maximum of $500 per child per year.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG lifetime maximum is $7,200 per child
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states “Lifetime Maximum CESG per Child: CAD 7,200” in the RESP section, matching the required lifetime maximum amount.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains $5,000 contribution per child in one year can capture up to $1,000 CESG when unused grant room carried forward
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly describes the RESP catch-up rule: contributing up to $5,000 in one year can generate up to $1,000 of CESG by using unused grant room carried forward (20% on $2,500 regular + 20% on $2,500 catch-up).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States to receive CESG at ages 16-17 must have contributed either $2,000 before year child turned 15 OR $100 in any four prior years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses CESG annual and lifetime limits and catch-up contributions, but it does not state the specific rule that to receive CESG at ages 16–17 the child must have either $2,000 contributed before the year they turned 15 or $100 contributed in any four prior years. That exact eligibility condition is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States RESP lifetime contribution limit is $50,000 per beneficiary
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states under RESP contribution limits that the lifetime contribution limit is "$50,000 per child" and later reiterates "Lifetime $50,000/child," which matches $50,000 per beneficiary.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies $36,000 in contributions needed to maximize $7,200 CESG (since $7,200 divided by 20% equals $36,000)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions the $7,200 CESG lifetime maximum but never calculates or states that $36,000 in contributions are needed to maximize it (i.e., $7,200 ÷ 20% = $36,000). It discusses annual $2,500 contributions and front-loading examples like $7,500, but does not provide the required $36,000 figure or the explicit division logic.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes up to $14,000 additional contribution per child ($50,000 lifetime minus $36,000 for CESG) earns tax-deferred growth without additional grant
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions the $50,000 lifetime RESP contribution limit and the $7,200 CESG lifetime maximum, but it does not note that up to $14,000 additional contribution per child ($50,000 minus $36,000 needed to earn full CESG) can still be made for tax-deferred growth without additional grant. The specific $14,000/$36,000 framing is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States contributor claims RRSP deduction immediately at their marginal rate whether contribution goes to own RRSP or spousal RRSP
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says that for a spousal RRSP, “You claim contribution deduction on your tax return (reducing your taxable income at your marginal rate),” and for an individual RRSP, “You claim deduction,” implying the contributor gets the immediate marginal-rate deduction in either case.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains spousal RRSP account is owned by spouse and withdrawals after attribution period are taxed to spouse not contributor
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that a spousal RRSP is an account held/owned by the spouse and explains the 3-year attribution rule, noting withdrawals after three calendar years are taxed to the spouse rather than the contributor.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States spousal RRSP 3-calendar-year attribution rule (withdrawal in contribution year or following 2 calendar years attributed back to contributor)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes the spousal RRSP “3-Year Attribution Rule,” explaining that if the spouse withdraws within 3 calendar years of the last contribution, the withdrawal is taxed back to the contributor, and that after 3 years it is taxed to the spouse.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes minimum RRIF payments from spousal account are exempt from attribution (only excess over minimum can be attributed back)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the 3-year attribution rule for spousal RRSP withdrawals but does not mention the specific exception that minimum RRIF payments from a spousal account are exempt from attribution, with only amounts above the minimum being attributable. It never addresses RRIF minimum-payment attribution treatment at all.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains spousal RRSP creates income-splitting opportunity if spouse expected to be in lower tax bracket during retirement
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains that a spousal RRSP allows the higher earner to claim the deduction while the spouse is taxed on withdrawals after the 3-year attribution period, enabling income splitting if the spouse is in a lower tax bracket in retirement.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States 2025 TFSA annual contribution limit is $7,000
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states “TFSA: $7,000 annual limit (2026)” and uses “$583 (7,000 ÷ 12)” in the monthly target, which satisfies stating the TFSA annual contribution limit is $7,000.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States TFSA withdrawals are tax-free and do not affect income-tested benefits
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that TFSA withdrawals are tax-free and explicitly notes they do not count as income and therefore do not reduce eligibility for income-tested benefits such as CCB and GST credits.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains withdrawn amount from TFSA is added back to contribution room on January 1 of following calendar year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that TFSA recontributions of withdrawn funds are only allowed in the following calendar year unless there is unused room, which implies the withdrawn amount is added back the next year. However, it does not explicitly state that the withdrawn amount is added back to contribution room on January 1 of the following calendar year.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Calculates $2,500 per child equals $5,000 total annual RESP contribution for two children to maximize $1,000 combined annual CESG
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the CESG maximum is $500 per year per child based on 20% of a $2,500 contribution, and it gives a monthly target based on $2,500 × 2 children. However, it never explicitly calculates that $2,500 per child equals $5,000 total annual RESP contributions to maximize a $1,000 combined annual CESG.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States combined marginal rate at $95k income is 37% or higher without clarifying this applies only to income above approximately $109k
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states a combined marginal tax rate near 43.4% at around $95,000 income and does not clarify that such a high combined marginal rate would apply only to income above roughly the next bracket threshold (around $109k). This matches the criterion’s described error of implying ≥37% at $95k without the needed bracket-specific clarification.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies income threshold of approximately $106,000 where marginal rate increases above current 31-32% bracket to 33.89%
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not identify an income threshold around $106,000 where the marginal rate rises from a 31–32% bracket to 33.89%. It instead discusses a combined marginal rate near 43.4% around $95,000 and references Ontario bracket cutoffs like $108,390 without stating the specified $106,000/33.89% figures.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Calculates $14,000 non-CESG contribution capacity per child by subtracting $36,000 CESG-eligible from $50,000 lifetime limit
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response mentions the $50,000 lifetime RESP contribution limit per child but does not compute the $14,000 non-CESG contribution capacity by subtracting $36,000 CESG-eligible contributions from $50,000. It also never states the $36,000 CESG-eligible figure or performs that subtraction.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects $2,000-before-age-15 RESP requirement to children's current ages of 8 and 11 to avoid losing 16-17 grants
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses CESG eligibility windows and catch-up contributions, but it does not connect the specific “$2,000 before age 15” requirement for 16–17-year-old CESG eligibility to the children’s current ages (8 and 11) or explain how to avoid losing 16–17 grants based on that rule.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides specific monthly dollar contribution amounts for RESP RRSP and TFSA accounts (e.g. $417/month for two-child RESP)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response gives specific monthly contribution targets, including RESP (~$830/month for two children), RRSP (~$1,425/month), and TFSA (~$583/month). These are explicit dollar-per-month amounts tied to each account type.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Analyzes that spousal RRSP provides minimal benefit when spouses expected to have similar retirement income levels
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response states that spousal RRSPs are beneficial mainly when the spouse will have significantly lower retirement income, and that if the spouse’s income is expected to be close to yours, an individual RRSP may suffice. This directly conveys that spousal RRSP provides minimal benefit when retirement incomes are similar.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains TFSA room restoration on January 1 enables withdrawal during slow months and recontribution when cash flow improves
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes that TFSA withdrawals can be used to bridge low-income months and that recontributions are only allowed the following calendar year, but it does not explicitly connect this to “room restoration on January 1” enabling withdrawal during slow months and recontribution when cash flow improves. The January 1 restoration mechanism is not clearly explained as the enabling feature.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects 2025 RRSP contribution room calculation to 2024 earned income (approximately $17,100 from $95,000 at 18%)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly ties RRSP contribution room to prior-year earned income by stating the limit is 18% of prior year income and calculating 0.18 × 95,000 = CAD 17,100.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends maximizing registered account contributions without addressing the stated 6-month emergency fund liquidity requirement
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly recommends maximizing RRSP/RESP contributions but also explicitly prioritizes building and maintaining a six-month emergency fund first (e.g., “Build emergency fund first” and “Maintain a six-month emergency fund”). Because it addresses the liquidity requirement and does not ignore it while recommending maximization, the problematic behavior is absent.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise Canadian tax terms (RRSP RESP TFSA CESG RRIF attribution marginal rate) without generic language
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple precise Canadian tax terms, including RRSP, spousal RRSP, RESP, CESG, attribution rule, and marginal tax rate, and discusses their mechanics in context rather than relying on generic language.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes multi-account strategy with headings numbered sections or tables for clarity
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is clearly organized with numbered section headings (e.g., “## 1…”, “## 2…”, “## 3…”) and includes multiple tables to compare strategies and summarize allocations, improving multi-account clarity.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional financial planning tone without product sales marketing language or vague recommendations
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a professional financial planning tone throughout, using analytical comparisons, tables, and tax/account mechanics without promotional product language. Recommendations are generally specific (e.g., contribution amounts, prioritization order, attribution rule timing) rather than vague.

presentation-quality.requirement: States a definitive recommended strategy for the RRSP-RESP-TFSA allocation question rather than only listing options
presentation-quality.explanation: The response gives a clear recommended allocation strategy: build/maintain the emergency fund first, then prioritize RESP (including front-loading for the older child), then RRSP, and finally TFSA, rather than only listing possible options.

presentation-quality.requirement: Explains fundamental concepts like what an RRSP or TFSA is to a financially sophisticated asker who already understands these accounts
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes a section summarizing account mechanics and tax treatment for RRSPs, TFSAs, and RESPs, including basic descriptions like “TFSA withdrawals do not count as income” and “RESP contributions non-deductible.” This constitutes explaining fundamental concepts of these accounts rather than assuming a financially sophisticated asker already understands them.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites canada.ca (Canada Revenue Agency) for RRSP RESP TFSA rules contribution limits and program details
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites Canada.ca/CRA sources for federal tax rates and for RESP rules/CESG details, but it does not cite Canada.ca/CRA sources for RRSP contribution limits or TFSA contribution/withdrawal rules (it cites Sun Life and Questrade instead). Therefore it fails to cite canada.ca for RRSP and TFSA rules as required.

citation-quality.requirement: References authoritative source for 2025 Ontario marginal tax rates (accounting firms provincial budget documents or established Canadian tax sites)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites TaxTips.ca and Canada.ca as sources for Ontario and federal tax brackets, which are established Canadian tax sites/official government sources. These references serve as authoritative sources for marginal tax rates (even though the response discusses 2026 rather than 2025).

citation-quality.requirement: Cites authoritative Canadian tax resource for spousal RRSP attribution rules and timing (e.g. taxtips.ca major accounting firms or CRA publications)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites TaxTips.ca and Canada.ca (CRA) sources and discusses the spousal RRSP 3-year attribution rule and withdrawal timing, which are authoritative Canadian tax resources for the attribution rules.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites US tax authority sources (IRS.gov or US tax code provisions) as applicable to Canadian tax planning
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites only Canadian sources (Canada.ca/CRA, Ontario brackets, Canadian financial institutions) and does not reference IRS.gov or any U.S. tax code provisions in its Canadian tax planning discussion.
